title: Classes & Objects
summary: Build your own type by keeping related data and behavior together.
refresher: A class is a blueprint for objects. Fields store each object's data. A constructor sets up a new object. Methods describe what the object can do. `new` creates an object from the class.

blocks.body: A class lets you create your own kind of value. Instead of passing around a loose name, id, and GPA, you can make one Student object that keeps those facts together.
Fields are the data each object carries. A constructor runs when you create a new object and fills in the starting values. Methods are actions that belong with that object.

blocks.caption: The constructor fills the fields. The displayName method uses the data stored inside this Student.

blocks.body: `new Student(...)` creates one object. If you call it again, you get a separate object with its own field values. Changing one student does not change the other.

blocks.title: You have been writing classes all along
blocks.body: Python lets you add attributes more freely. Java wants the class to say its fields up front. That feels strict at first, but it helps Java catch misspelled or missing data early.

Setting each field by hand after `new` gets tedious, and it is easy to forget one. A constructor does it for you. It is a method with the same name as the class and no return type, and it runs automatically as part of `new`.

blocks.caption: Methods are the behavior half of the blueprint, the way fields are the state half. Note that changing `grace` leaves `ada` untouched: they are two separate objects built from one class, each with its own copy of the fields.
blocks.output:
Ada has a GPA of 3.9
Grace has a GPA of 2.5
Grace has a GPA of 3.1
Ada has a GPA of 3.9

blocks.title: What `this` is for
blocks.body: In that constructor, the parameter is called `name` and the field is also called `name`. Java resolves the plain name `name` to the parameter, because the closer one wins. So `this.name = name` means: take the value that was passed in, and store it in this object's field. Drop the `this.` and you write `name = name`, which quietly assigns the parameter to itself and leaves the field empty. It compiles. It just does nothing.

blocks.why: `new` was called once, so there is exactly one object. `Student b = a;` copies the reference, not the object, so `a` and `b` are two names pointing at the same Student. Changing it through `b` changes what you see through `a`, and the answer is 2.0. If you picked 3.9, you read `b = a` as making a second student. It does not: only `new` builds an object, and there is no second `new` here.

blocks.body: Now for the `static` in `public static void main`. A static method belongs to the class itself, not to any object. That is exactly what `main` needs to be, because `main` is where your program starts. At that moment no objects exist yet, so there is nothing for a non-static method to be attached to. `main` runs first and builds the objects.

blocks.caption: `describe()` is an instance method: it needs a Student to describe. `main` is static, so it has no Student. Java catches this while compiling, before a single line runs. Nothing prints. You get: `error: non-static method describe() cannot be referenced from a static context`. The fix is to build an object first and call the method on it: `new Student().describe()`.

blocks.body: That is worth pausing on, because it is a real difference from Python. Java checks types and method calls while compiling, so a mistake like this is caught before the program runs at all. An error that arrives with no output above it is a compile error, and it means the program never started. An error that arrives after some of your output has already printed is a runtime error, and it means the program was running fine until that line.

blocks.caption: Both sides compile, which is what makes this one sting: the mistake survives the compiler and only shows up when you run it. Declaring a variable of type Student does not create a Student, so the NullPointerException side holds a reference pointing at nothing. The dot in `s.name` says "go to the object and get its name", and there is no object to go to.

blocks.title: A NullPointerException means you never called new
blocks.body: This is the error Java beginners hit most. It does not mean the object is broken. It means there is no object. Some reference is holding `null`, and you used a dot on it. Java tells you which line, so go to that line, find the variable before the dot, and trace backwards to where it should have been given a `new`. Usually you declared it in one branch of an `if` and used it outside.

blocks.prompt: The Student class declares `String name;` and `double gpa;` and has no constructor. What does this print?
choices: It throws a NullPointerException on the first println
It does not compile, since the fields were never set
blocks.why: `new` really did build an object, so `s` points at something and the dot works. Java fills unset fields with defaults: a `double` starts at 0.0, and any object type, including `String`, starts at `null`. So you get `null` and then `0.0`. If you picked the NullPointerException, you had the right idea about `null` but the wrong target: `s.name` is null, and `s` is not. Printing a null is fine. Only using a dot on a null crashes.

blocks.title: Working with a new class
items: Use a class when data belongs together.
Use new to create an object.
